hints.junior: setTimeout(0) doesn't mean 'now'; it means 'after the current task and all microtasks finish.' That ordering is what trips everyone up.
hints.mid: The execution order surprise is always: sync → microtasks → macrotask. Run the console snippet: seeing it once makes it permanent.
hints.staff: The surprise is usually microtask flood starving the renderer. Check LoAF entries in Chrome 123+; they show the full frame cost including microtask drain.

hints.junior: Click Blocking first, count in your head. The counter freezes because JavaScript is busy. Then click Yielding and see the difference.
hints.mid: Watch the counter rate in yielding mode. Each chunk yield gives the renderer a chance to update: that's how smooth progress UIs stay smooth.
hints.staff: Open DevTools Performance → record while clicking Blocking. Look for a red Long Task bar. Click Yielding: many short green tasks instead.

hints.junior: The fix is just two lines: wrap the chunk in an async function and add await setTimeout(0). That's it.
hints.mid: scheduler.yield() is the modern version: it's in Chrome 94+ and cooperates with the browser's built-in priority system.
hints.staff: Reach for Web Workers when work is consistently >1s or involves binary data (WASM, ImageData). Chunk-yielding is for sporadic bursts of JS computation.

hints.junior: Think of JavaScript as a chef with one pair of hands: it finishes one order (a macrotask), clears every urgent ticket that came in during it (microtasks), then moves to the next order — urgent tickets always jump the line.
hints.mid: The spec's actual loop shape: run the oldest macrotask to completion, fully drain the microtask queue even if it keeps growing, paint if a frame is due, repeat. A Promise chain that keeps re-enqueuing itself can starve rendering entirely.
hints.staff: Long Animation Frames (LoAF) in Chrome DevTools now surfaces exactly when a task plus its full microtask drain exceeds 50ms — your real production signal for event loop contention, not just guessing from a janky scroll.

anecdotes.junior: I once spent a day debugging why a button click handler wasn't working: it was a microtask flood from a Promise chain locking up the input listener.
anecdotes.mid: The first time I saw the microtask/macrotask distinction it felt arcane. Then I fixed a janky animation by moving one line before a await: instant insight.
anecdotes.staff: Org-wide: when teams add React.startTransition for input responsiveness without understanding why it works, they eventually regress it. The event loop mental model makes the why obvious.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're solving that mystery where setTimeout zero doesn't actually run immediately. Once you see why, a whole category of bugs disappears.
sections.mid: This case is about the JavaScript event loop, specifically why microtasks run before macrotasks, and how that affects rendering and input responsiveness.
sections.staff: We're covering the event loop's three queues, the production observability toolchain, and when to reach for Web Workers versus yielding on the main thread.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle: read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: Click Blocking first, count in your head. The counter freezes because JavaScript is busy. Then click Yielding and see the difference.
sections.mid: Watch the counter rate in yielding mode. Each chunk yield gives the renderer a chance to update: that's how smooth progress UIs stay smooth.
sections.staff: Open DevTools Performance → record while clicking Blocking. Look for a red Long Task bar. Click Yielding: many short green tasks instead.
